3: apethanete gar kai he zoe hymon kekryptai syn to Christo en to theo (apethanete gar kai hē zōē hymōn kekryptai syn tō Christō en tō theō): 'for you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God.' The perfect passive kekryptai (is and remains hidden) describes the present state: the believer's true life is concealed within the divine-Christological reality, not visible to external observation. This 'hiddenness' will be resolved at the parousia (v. 4: 'when Christ who is your life appears, you also will appear with him in glory') — eschatological glory is the unveiling of what is now hidden. The mystical union language ('with Christ in God') positions the believer's ontological security within the relationship between the Son and the Father.
1: Ei oun synēgerthēte tō Christō, ta anō zēteite, hou ho Christos estin en dexia tou theou kathēmenos (if then you were raised with Christ, seek the things above, where Christ is, seated at the right hand of God): co-resurrection with Christ creates a new orientation. Ta anō (things above) is not Platonic otherworldliness but eschatological priority — what matters in the light of Christ's reign. En dexia tou theou kathēmenos (seated at the right hand of God) — Ps 110:1, the most-cited OT text in the NT; Christ's session frames all cosmic-rule claims in the letter.
2: Ta anō phroneite, mē ta epi tēs gēs (set your minds on things above, not on earthly things): phroneite (set your minds/think) — the verb of cognitive and volitional orientation; the same root as phronēsis (practical wisdom). The contrast 'above/earthly' is eschatological, not ontological: what is above is the realm of the ascended Christ; what is earthly is the passing age's value-system.
4: Hotan ho Christos phanerōthē, hē zōē hymōn, tote kai hymeis syn autō phanerōthēsesthe en doxē (when Christ, your life, is revealed, then you also will be revealed with him in glory): the Parousia as disclosure (phanerōsis) of what is now hidden. Hē zōē hymōn (your life) — Christ is identified with the believer's life, a participatory depth comparable to John 14:6 ('I am the life'). Eschatological glory is the unveiling of what is now hidden in Christ.
5: Nekrōsate oun ta melē ta epi tēs gēs: porneian, akatharsia, pathos, epithumian kakēn, kai tēn pleonexian hētis estin eidōlolatria (put to death your members that are on the earth: sexual immorality, impurity, passion, evil desire, and greed which is idolatry): the imperative nekrōsate (put to death) follows from the indicative of co-death with Christ (2:20; 3:3). Five vices culminating in pleonexia identified as idolatry — the worship of having over worship of God. The sequence moves from outward acts to inward desires to the root disposition.
6: Di' ha erchetai hē orgē tou theou (because of these the wrath of God is coming): orgē tou theou (wrath of God) — in Paul, not arbitrary anger but the structured divine consequence of suppressing truth and choosing creature over Creator (Rom 1:18-32). The present tense erchetai signals the certainty of future judgment: the vices are not merely socially harmful but cosmically accountable.
7: En hois kai hymeis periepatesate pote, hote ezēte en toutois (in which also you once walked, when you were living in these things): the past tense (periepatesate/ezēte) frames pre-conversion life as genuinely past. The change is ontological (1:13: transferred from one dominion to another) rather than merely moral. The vice list is not theoretical but autobiographical for Gentile readers who lived within these patterns.
8: Nyni de apothesthe kai hymeis ta panta: orgēn, thymon, kakian, blasphēmian, aischrologian ek tou stomatos hymōn (but now also put off all these things: anger, rage, malice, slander, obscene speech from your mouth): a second vice list, now relational. Orgē (settled anger), thymos (passionate outburst), kakia (ill-will), blasphēmia (defamatory speech), aischrologia (obscene speech). The progression maps inner states (orgē, thymos, kakia) to their verbal expression (blasphēmia, aischrologia).
9: Mē pseudesthe eis allēlous, apekdysamenoi ton palaion anthrōpon syn tais praxesin autou (do not lie to each other, having stripped off the old man with his practices): apekdysamenoi (having stripped off) — the aorist participle refers to the once-for-all act at conversion/baptism; the imperative builds on it. Palaios anthrōpos (old man) — the pre-conversion self as an entire mode of existence, not merely bad habits. The clothing metaphor (strip/put on) continues through v.14.
10: Kai endysamenoi ton neon ton anakainoumenon eis epignōsin kat' eikona tou ktisantos auton (and having put on the new, which is being renewed unto knowledge according to the image of the one who created it): anakainoumenon (being renewed) — present passive: ongoing renewal. Kat' eikona tou ktisantos auton (according to the image of the one who created it) — the new creation restores the imago Dei (Gen 1:26-27), distorted at the Fall. Christ as the image of God (1:15) is the template; the believer is progressively re-imaged toward the Creator's original intention.
11: Hopou ouk eni Hellēn kai Ioudaios, peritomē kai akrobystia, barbaros, Skythēs, doulos, eleutheros, alla panta kai en pasin Christos (where there is neither Greek nor Jew, circumcised or uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave or free, but Christ is all and in all): the new humanity transcends the three primary social binaries: ethnic (Greek/Jew), religio-cultural (circumcised/uncircumcised), and social-economic (slave/free). The Scythian — in Greco-Roman ethnography the nadir of civilization — is explicitly included. Panta kai en pasin Christos (Christ is all and in all) — the most comprehensive inclusion formula in Paul.
12: Endysasthe oun, hōs eklektoi tou theou hagioi kai ēgapēmenoi, splanchna oiktirmou, chrēstotēta, tapeinophrosunēn, prautēta, makrothymian (put on, as chosen ones of God, holy and beloved, heart of compassion, kindness, humble-mindedness, gentleness, long-suffering): the virtue list is structured by communal identity (eklektoi, hagioi, ēgapēmenoi — election vocabulary from Deuteronomy and the Psalms). Splanchna oiktirmou (heart of compassion) — splanchna (viscera) is the seat of deep gut-level emotion in Hellenistic and Jewish anthropology.
13: Charizomenoi heautois, ean tis pros tina echē momphēn: kathōs kai ho Kyrios echarisato hymin, houtōs kai hymeis (forgiving one another, if anyone has a complaint against someone: just as the Lord forgave you, so also you): charizomenoi (forgiving/graciously giving) — from charis; to forgive is to extend grace. The Christological standard: the Lord's prior forgiveness of the believers establishes the mode of their forgiveness of each other — not contractual reciprocity but grace-precedent.
14: Epi pasi de toutois tēn agapēn, ho estin syndesmos tēs teleiotētos (and over all these things love, which is the bond of completeness): syndesmos tēs teleiotētos (bond of completeness/perfection) — agapē is the integrating virtue that holds all other virtues together, as a belt holds a garment. Teleiotētos (completeness, maturity) connects to the letter's goal of presenting everyone teleios (1:28); love is what completes the virtue structure.
15: Kai hē eirēnē tou Christou brabeuetō en tais kardiais hymōn, eis hēn kai eklēthēte en heni sōmati: kai eucharistoi ginesthe (and let the peace of Christ umpire in your hearts, to which also you were called in one body: and become thankful): brabeuetō (umpire/decide) — the athletic umpire who adjudicates disputed contests; the peace of Christ settles community conflicts. Eucharistoi ginesthe (become thankful) — the imperative of becoming: an ongoing cultivation of habitual gratitude.
16: Ho logos tou Christou enoikeito en hymin plousiōs en pasē sophia didaskontes kai nouthetountes heautous psalmois hymnois ōdais pneumatikais en [tē] chariti adontes en [tais] kardiais hymōn tō theō (let the word of Christ dwell in you richly in all wisdom, teaching and admonishing one another with psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, singing in grace in your hearts to God): enoikeito en hymin plousiōs (dwell in you richly) — enoikeō (dwell within); the word takes up permanent residence in the community. The three song-types (psalmos, hymnos, ōdē pneumatikē) suggest the full range of the community's corporate worship as the primary medium of word-formation.
17: Kai pan ho ti ean poiēte en logō ē en ergō, panta en onomati Kyriou Iēsou, eucharistountes tō theō patri di' autou (and whatever you do, in word or deed, all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him): en onomati Kyriou Iēsou (in the name of the Lord Jesus) — to act in Christ's name is to act as his representative under his lordship. This verse sets the theological principle governing the household code (3:18–4:1): the Kyrios who is the standard for all conduct is Christ, not Caesar or social convention.
18: Hai gynaikes, hypotassesthe tois andrasin hymōn, hōs anēken en Kyriō (wives, submit yourselves to your own husbands, as is fitting in the Lord): the haustafeln (household code) adapts the Hellenistic household management schema (Aristotle's Politics 1.5) with Christological qualifiers. Hypotassesthe — middle/reflexive voice: voluntary ordering of oneself within a relational structure. Hōs anēken en Kyriō (as is fitting in the Lord) — Christ's lordship both frames and potentially limits any conventional household obligation.
19: Hoi andres, agapaite tas gynaikas kai mē pikrainesthe pros autas (husbands, love your wives and do not be harsh/bitter toward them): the husband's obligation is active love (agapaite), not the exercise of legal authority. The prohibition of pikrainesthe (bitterness/harshness — pikros = sharp, bitter) restricts the exercise of power that would make the relationship toxic. The household code within the Christ-frame reshapes power dynamics toward mutual service.
20: Ta tekna, hypakouete tois goneusin kata panta, touto gar euareston estin en Kyriō (children, obey your parents in all things, for this is pleasing in the Lord): hypakouete (obey) is stronger than hypotassesthe (submit) — children obey, wives voluntarily submit. Euareston estin en Kyriō (is pleasing in the Lord) — the standard is the Lord's pleasure, not merely social convention, placing a Christological qualifier on the conventional expectation.
21: Hoi pateres, mē erethizete ta tekna hymōn, hina mē athumōsin (fathers, do not provoke/exasperate your children, so that they will not become discouraged): in the Greco-Roman patria potestas system, the father had absolute legal authority over children including life and death. Paul's counter-injunction limits paternal power by the child's interior state: fathering that produces athumia (despondency/loss of spirit) violates the lordship of Christ. This directly restricts ancient paternal authority.
22: Hoi douloi, hypakouete kata panta tois kata sarka kyriois, mē en ophthalmodouleia hōs anthrōpareskoi, all' en haplotēti kardias phoboumenoi ton Kyrion (slaves, obey your earthly masters in all things, not in eye-service as people-pleasers, but in singleness of heart fearing the Lord): ophthalmodouleia (eye-service) — Paul may have coined this compound; service performed only when watched. En haplotēti kardias (in singleness/simplicity of heart) — undivided, wholehearted engagement. The ultimate accountability is to the divine Kyrios, not the human master.
23: Ho ean poiēte, ek psychēs ergazesthe, hōs tō Kyriō kai ouk anthrōpois (whatever you do, work from the soul, as to the Lord and not to humans): ek psychēs (from the soul/whole being) — not reluctant compliance but wholehearted engagement. The reorientation of the work motive to the divine Kyrios transforms the experience of coerced slave labor by relocating the ultimate accountability relationship — a profound existential shift within an unjust social system.
24: Apolēmpsesthe tēn antapodosis tēs klēronomiās: tō Kyriō Christō douleuete (you will receive the repayment of the inheritance: you are serving the Lord Christ): antapodosis tēs klēronomiās (repayment of the inheritance) — a paradox: inheritance is received by birthright, not earned; 'repayment' is merited. The combination destabilizes both categories: the eschatological inheritance is guaranteed by grace but distributed in correspondence to faithful service. The slave's true Kyrios is not the human master but Christ the divine Lord.
25: Ho gar adikōn komisetai ho ēdikēsen, kai ouk estin prosōpolēmpsia (for the one who does wrong will receive back what wrong he did, and there is no partiality): komisetai ho ēdikēsen (will receive back the wrong done) — exact correspondence of wrong and consequence. Prosōpolēmpsia (partiality/face-receiving) — literally 'receiving the face' as a Semitic idiom for status-based favoritism. God's judgment is impartial: slave and master face the same standard. A quiet but radical social leveler within the household code.
